pattern: A single bit-flip on one array element toggles exactly that bit in the running XOR of the whole array, so the cheapest way to reach the target XOR is to flip precisely the bit positions where the current combined XOR and the target disagree.
hints: Combine every element with XOR first -- notice that flipping one bit of one element changes the overall XOR by exactly that same bit, nothing more.
Compare the combined XOR of the array against k: the positions where they differ are exactly the bits you must flip, and each one costs exactly one operation.
Count the set bits of (combined XOR of nums) XOR k -- that count is the answer, no simulation of actual flips needed.

edge_inputs.description: combined XOR already equals k, zero operations needed
typical small array needing a couple of bit flips
single-element array, k equal to that element
single-element array of zero against a nonzero k
k at the upper bound of the allowed range
every element is zero, so the answer is exactly the bit count of k itself
k is zero, so the answer is just the bit count of the array's own combined XOR
combined XOR and k differ in every low bit, forcing several flips

stress.description: large random array with a random target k across the full allowed range
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 100000))
    nums = [rng.randint(0, 1000000) for _ in range(n)]
    k = rng.randint(0, 1000000)
    return [nums, k]

stress.description: many zeros mixed with a few large values, k chosen independently
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 5000))
    nums = [0 if rng.random() < 0.7 else rng.randint(0, 1000000) for _ in range(n)]
    k = rng.randint(0, 1000000)
    return [nums, k]
